story:
My husband plans like it is breathing. He time-blocks each day on crisp spreadsheets, every minute accounted for. Sunday mornings are his ritual: the click-clack of keys, coffee going cold, a week carved into boxes. We’ve been married seven years. Everyone says marriage is compromise, but with him it feels like conformity—to his structure, his careful, measured life. He is a good man, a devoted father of two, steady and kind. He schedules family time, even makes notes on what to ask us. And yet our life feels dull and grey to me, while I crave yellow and bright orange—spontaneity, surprise, stolen kisses, the thrill of plans that aren’t plans at all. He clings to order like a child to a mother’s skirt. A small change means recalculating, rewriting, saving a lesson for next time. I am the opposite: I follow the river and the wind, turn where the path twists, and trust the joy at the bend. I don’t want to be another task he completes. I want to be the light dancing in his eyes, a bright star in his sky. I love him, I do.

I wake before the sun on Sundays, boil the kettle, stir instant coffee, and open Excel. I review last week, list what went well, what to improve. Failure to plan is planning to fail, I remind myself. I must not fail—at work, at home, as a husband and father. This week is heavy with meetings and deadlines. I’ll submit the reports, then fit family time in Friday evening. It hurts to push them later, but I’m doing this for them. I love them, I do. My father was strict. If I was late, I paid. I learned early: to be safe, you must be prepared; to be loved, you must be perfect. Discipline etched itself into me until planning became my Sunday worship. Success meant control; failure was a door I dared not open. I watch my wife now, see the fading shine in her eyes, and step carefully. I try to think of everything—clear the path, carry the weight, shield her from the unexpected storm. I schedule time for her, prepare words to say, make sure there’s nothing to worry her. But why does it feel like I’m failing anyway? What am I not seeing? I want to be the steady beat of her heart, the arm she trusts to hold her. I am not my father. I will make room for joy, for love, for our small, ordinary delights—and I will plan for that too, to give us the best chance at happiness. Still, I wonder: Is there a flaw in my perfect grid? What steals her joy? What does her smile hide? I don’t know how to fix this.

We stand on the same shore, watching different tides. She longs for surprise; I long for certainty. We both reach, and keep missing. We both promise, and still feel alone. We both love each other, we do.
